Sample Digest · Monday 27 April 2026

A recent Radiology digest,
as sent to subscribers

This is the real Monday digest from Monday 27 April 2026. Showing the first 8 of 21 articles from that week — every new paper from the top journals in Radiology.

21articles that week
4journals covered
£0for your first 4 weeks
Note: Summaries are AI-generated from publicly available PubMed abstracts. Always verify against the source article before clinical use.
AJR. American journal of roentgenology

Interreader Agreement for Individual Ancillary Features in LI-RADS CT/MRI Version 2018: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis

Jung YJ, Choi SH, Heo S et al. · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Systematic review and meta-analysis
Key Question: What is the interreader agreement for individual ancillary features in LI-RADS CT/MRI version 2018?
Key Findings:
  • Analysis of 47 studies (11,891 patients, 13,695 observations) showed variable agreement across 18 reported ancillary features, with kappa values ranging from 0.26 to 0.84
  • Features favouring malignancy demonstrated higher agreement (kappa 0.58-0.84) than those favouring benignity (kappa 0.26-0.73)
  • Enhancement paralleling blood pool showed poorest agreement (kappa 0.26), while hepatobiliary-phase hypointensity showed best agreement (kappa 0.84)
Clinical Relevance: This evidence highlights significant interpretation variability in LI-RADS ancillary features that UK radiologists rely upon for hepatocellular carcinoma diagnosis, potentially impacting diagnostic confidence and patient management decisions.
Limitations: Three ancillary features were not reported in any included studies, limiting the comprehensiveness of the agreement assessment.
PubMed →DOI →
AJR. American journal of roentgenology

Shaping Digital Health Policy-Career Pivots, an AJR Podcast Series (Episode 10)

Dogra S, Juluru K · 2026 Apr 22
Study Type: Podcast/Commentary
Key Question: How do career transitions into digital health policy development occur and what insights can be gained for radiologists considering this path?
Key Findings:
  • This is a podcast episode focusing on career pivots into digital health policy rather than original research
  • No quantitative findings or clinical data are provided in the abstract
  • The content appears to explore professional transitions and policy development perspectives
Clinical Relevance: May provide guidance for UK radiologists interested in transitioning from clinical practice to digital health policy roles, particularly relevant given NHS digital transformation initiatives and policy development needs.
Limitations: Abstract provides insufficient detail to assess the specific content, methodology, or evidence base of the podcast discussion.
PubMed →DOI →
European radiology

Staging of non-metastatic nasopharyngeal carcinoma with PET/CT

Cao C, Treechairusame T, Wu Y et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: Does the ninth edition AJCC/UICC TNM staging system accurately predict overall survival in non-metastatic nasopharyngeal carcinoma patients staged with PET/CT?
Key Findings:
  • TNM-9 showed declining 5-year overall survival rates across stages (Stage I: 91.7%, II: 84.8%, III: 76.8%), but differences were not statistically significant (p=0.090)
  • TNM-9 had inferior discriminatory performance compared to TNM-8 (AUC 0.6239 vs 0.6678, p=0.0003)
  • Nodal classifications (N0-N3) showed no significant survival differences (p=0.879)
Clinical Relevance: UK radiologists staging nasopharyngeal carcinoma with PET/CT should be aware that the current TNM-9 system may have limited prognostic value, potentially affecting treatment stratification and multidisciplinary team discussions.
Limitations: Single-centre retrospective study spanning 20 years may introduce treatment era bias and limit generalisability to current UK practice.
PubMed →DOI →
European radiology

Reporting efficiency in diagnostic imaging: Can plug-and-play general-purpose large language models outperform conventional speech recognition?

de Margerie-Mellon C, Duron L, Fournier L et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Prospective multicenter comparative study
Key Question: Does a general-purpose large language model with speech recognition improve reporting efficiency and accuracy compared to conventional speech recognition in routine radiology practice?
Key Findings:
  • LLM reduced median report generation time by 25% overall (238 vs 318 seconds, p<0.01), though only 3 of 5 radiologists experienced time savings
  • LLM significantly reduced grammar/spelling errors (79 vs 293) and transcription errors (225 vs 445) compared to conventional speech recognition
  • LLM introduced new error patterns including 99 instances of non-compliance to instructions and 4 confabulations, with higher character-level changes (Levenshtein distance 43 vs 20)
Clinical Relevance: This technology could enhance NHS radiology workflow efficiency and reduce transcription errors, though benefits vary significantly between individual radiologists' working styles.
Limitations: Time savings were inconsistent across radiologists, and the study introduces new types of errors that may require additional quality assurance measures.
PubMed →DOI →
European radiology

Recruitment strategies and outcomes in lung cancer screening programmes: a scoping review

Djuric O, Venturelli F, Bassi MC et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Systematic scoping review
Key Question: What recruitment strategies are most effective for identifying and enrolling at-risk populations in lung cancer screening programmes using low-dose CT?
Key Findings:
  • Population-based programmes achieved highest screening coverage (35-40%) compared to volunteer-based (4.8%) or referral-based programmes (6.7%)
  • Participation rates varied widely: targeted programmes 8.4-55.9%, referral-based 30.9-92.5%, underserved populations 31-92%
  • No programme achieved high overall test coverage, with an inverse relationship between population reach and participation rates
Clinical Relevance: UK radiology departments planning lung cancer screening implementation need evidence-based recruitment strategies to optimise population coverage whilst maintaining acceptable participation rates in screening programmes.
Limitations: The review identified no single recruitment strategy that achieved both high population reach and high participation rates simultaneously.
PubMed →DOI →
European radiology

Detection of calcified plaques: comparison between coronary CT angiography and thin-slice non-contrast CT with deep learning-aided image registration

Schulze K, Biavati F, Föllmer B et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Retrospective comparative analysis
Key Question: Does coronary CT angiography miss calcified plaques that are detectable on thin-slice non-contrast CT?
Key Findings:
  • CCTA missed 37.6% (121/322) of calcified plaques detected by thin-slice NCCT, leading to misclassification of 8.9% of patients as having no plaques
  • Missed plaques were significantly smaller (median 3.0 vs 9.2 mm³, p<0.001) and lower density (median 212.7 vs 292.7 HU, p<0.001) than detected plaques
  • Standard calcium scoring CT detected only 44% of plaques missed by CCTA
Clinical Relevance: This suggests UK cardiac CT services may be underestimating plaque burden when relying on CCTA alone, potentially impacting cardiovascular risk stratification and treatment decisions.
Limitations: Small sample size (45 patients) from a single trial limits generalisability to routine clinical practice.
PubMed →DOI →
European radiology

Long-term outcomes of genicular artery embolization for knee osteoarthritis: 12-month efficacy and secondary outcomes from a randomized sham-controlled clinical trial

van Zadelhoff TA, van der Heijden RA, Bierma-Zeinstra SMA et al. · 2026 Apr 20
Study Type: Randomized sham-controlled clinical trial
Key Question: Is genicular artery embolization more effective than sham procedure for treating pain in mild to moderate knee osteoarthritis at 12 months?
Key Findings:
  • Both GAE and sham groups showed similar pain improvement (GAE: 44.4 to 65.6; sham: 42.3 to 58.2) with no significant between-group difference (7.46 points, 95% CI: -13.63 to -28.56, p=0.25)
  • No significant changes in MRI-assessed synovitis in either group at any timepoint
  • Sustained placebo effect observed up to 12 months in both groups
Clinical Relevance: These results challenge the efficacy of GAE for knee osteoarthritis and suggest interventional radiologists should not routinely offer this procedure, as benefits appear entirely placebo-driven.
Limitations: Small sample size (58 patients total) may limit generalizability of findings.
PubMed →DOI →
European radiology

From pixels to pathology: how artificial intelligence mammographic risk scores capture tumor biology through imaging

Zhang Z, Elabeid J, Ali T et al. · 2026 Apr 21
Study Type: Retrospective cohort study
Key Question: What clinical and pathological tumor characteristics are associated with AI-generated mammographic risk scores from the year prior to cancer detection?
Key Findings:
  • AI risk scores showed modest discrimination for predicting biopsy-confirmed cancer (AUC 0.62, 95% CI: 0.59-0.65)
  • Grade 3 tumors had significantly lower prior-year AI risk scores than Grade 1 tumors (p = 0.016)
  • Initial association between invasive lobular carcinoma and higher risk scores was mediated by tumor grade
Clinical Relevance: AI mammographic risk assessment may detect subtle imaging features of low-grade malignancies up to one year before clinical detection, potentially informing risk stratification in NHS breast screening programmes.
Limitations: Modest discriminative performance suggests limited clinical utility as a standalone risk assessment tool.
PubMed →DOI →
